# Questions in Livestream 65

Live Stream #65: The Storage City of Popcorn

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=414" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:54</a> | If the Bible says little about the physical origin of the universe, what does it say about the physical end of the universe? | He said the Bible gives only broad eschatological language: the present creation passes away, believers remain with God, but the exact physical state of the renewed creation is left vague. | He contrasted origins with eschatology by saying Scripture gives even less concrete physical detail about the end than about the beginning. It says believers will be with God, the old heavens and earth will be rolled up and replaced by new heavens and a new earth, and 2 Peter speaks of fire, but he stressed that the text does not explain what the final physical universe will be like. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=543" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:03</a> | What advice would he give amateur archaeologists who want to engage the material seriously without becoming professionals? | Read constantly. He said wide, disciplined reading of reports, articles, and books matters more than the formal credential by itself. | His answer was that serious engagement requires time spent reading archaeological reports, peer-reviewed articles, books, and collected essays. He said a well-read amateur can engage the material better than a credentialed archaeologist who stops reading, because effectiveness comes from sustained familiarity with the scholarly literature. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:08</a> | Are readings like `Heights of David` and `Enclosure of Abraham` on Shoshenq I's Bubastite Portal real? | He said the `Heights of David` reading is fairly strong, while the `Enclosure of Abraham` reading is possible but less certain because the sign group may also refer to `stallions`. | He said the `Heights of David` reading in rings 105-106 is backed by Kenneth Kitchen and by the way David's name appears in ancient documents, so he regards it as strong. The `Enclosure of Abraham` reading near Hebron is not wild, but he hedged it because the same signs could be read as an enclosure of stallions; he said he had checked the hieroglyphic text himself and found the claim mostly true, with the Abraham reading less secure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=866" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:26</a> | Are there ancient Egyptian or Near Eastern parallels to Joseph's silver divination cup in Genesis 44:5? | Yes. He pointed to Tutankhamun's alabaster divination cup as a real Egyptian example, while noting silver vessels were rarer and more likely to be recycled. | He identified Tutankhamun's alabaster cup as an extant Egyptian divination cup and described its lotus handles and Thoth imagery as fitting divinatory use. He noted that the surviving example is alabaster rather than silver, but silver was very rare and valuable in Egypt, so silver vessels were likely to be melted down and reused instead of surviving archaeologically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=1033" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:13</a> | Is the idea that the tabernacle was a huge circular yurt or dome plausible? | No. He dismissed it as an engineer's fantasy that does not fit the biblical description or the need for portability. | He recalled seeing a display for this theory at a scholarly meeting and said the proposed structure was an enormous dome, roughly imagined as a hundred feet across and high. His objection was that such a structure would not be portable and did not fit the text, so he treated it as an idealized structural-engineering fantasy rather than a plausible tabernacle reconstruction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=1192" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:52</a> | Are the genealogies in Matthew 1 and Luke 3 partly clan lists rather than only individual pedigrees? | Partly. He said the early portions that draw on Genesis should still be read largely in clan terms, while later portions move more toward individuals. | He divided the question around the Exodus, saying the genealogical material before that point is usually clan material while later lists begin moving toward named individuals. Because Matthew 1 and Luke 3 draw on Genesis for their earlier names, he thinks those early entries should mostly be read as clans, while the later portions are more individual. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=1307" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:47</a> | How should the Jericho dating debate between about 1550 BC and about 1400 BC be reconciled, especially with the scarab of Amenhotep III? | He argued there is no real contradiction: a Middle Bronze destruction fits around 1550, later Late Bronze occupation existed, and newer work at Jericho undercuts the idea that the site was simply destroyed by Joshua around 1400. | He said Kathleen Kenyon's 1550 date refers to the Middle Bronze destruction of City IV, while Bryant Wood's 1400 argument depends on reading later finds as proof that same destruction was Joshua's. He appealed to Lorenzo Nigro's work for Late Bronze fortifications and occupation, arguing that the Amenhotep III scarab and Mycenaean pottery make sense in a later city that survived into the thirteenth century before later leveling. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=1657" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:37</a> | Could mainstream scholarship eventually accept a historical Exodus and conquest without accepting the supernatural claims? | He thinks the field is already moving that way, with more scholars willing to treat the events historically while bracketing miraculous interpretation. | He said biblical minimalism was much stronger when he first studied archaeology, but the field now has less consensus and more room for historical readings. He compared Exodus language to Egyptian royal inscriptions filled with divine claims: a secular historian can accept that a migration and settlement happened without accepting the miracle claims, just as historians can accept historical elements in Ramesses II's Kadesh account without believing in Amun or Montu. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=1946" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:26</a> | Is there new archaeological evidence for Solomon or for Rehoboam's encounter with Shishak? | He said not much new has appeared recently, but he read the Shishak episode as pressure on Jerusalem that ended with a payoff rather than a full invasion of the city. | He said publication had been slow and did not point to much new evidence. On Shishak/Shoshenq I, he argued that Egyptian campaigns were mainly about extracting wealth; Rehoboam's payment meant Jerusalem did not need to be invaded, so Shishak could take the tribute and move on. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=2169" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:09</a> | If Adam's 930 years are really a clan lifespan, how should Genesis 5:5 be understood? | He argued the text reflects clan continuity rather than one individual's literal age, tying this to broader ancient Near Eastern ideas about lineage, memory, and the survival of a named house. | He said Hebrew grammar alone does not settle the issue because the missing piece is cultural context. He explained ancient ideas of bodily death, death of memory, and death of the name, then argued that a clan named for Adam could be said to live as long as its direct family line and identity continued, with Genesis and Exodus genealogies giving hints that long spans can refer to clan continuity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=2504" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:44</a> | Should we expect to find Moses' name in hieroglyphs, and what might that name have looked like in Egyptian? | He said it is debated, but preferred a theophoric Egyptian background over the simpler `ms/msy` explanation, and thought an Egyptian form behind `Moshe` is plausible. | He described two theories: the common `ms/msy` explanation meaning born or begotten, and a theophoric proposal involving a divine name followed by `m` and `shay`, related to river or lake language. He preferred the latter because the shift from Egyptian `s` to Hebrew shin in the simpler explanation seemed problematic to him, and he sketched how an Egyptian form could use an `m`, a pool sign, and a determinative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=2718" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:18</a> | What should be made of the so-called Samson seal from Tel Beth-Shemesh? | He said he knew of the claim but lacked enough provenance and excavation context to treat it confidently, so he would not build much on it. | He said he had heard of the seal and thought it involved a lion image, but he did not know enough about its provenance or whether it came from a dig or the antiquities market. He also noted that names and theophoric elements can be common, so without stronger context he would not use the seal as firm evidence for Samson. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=2838" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:18</a> | Could someone say `I am` in ancient Hebrew without making a divine claim? | Yes. He said ordinary forms for `I am` existed, and the force of passages like John 8:58 depends on the specific divine verbal form being invoked. | He said ordinary Hebrew could say `I am` without offense because the regular verbal form differs from the divine-name form, especially in the third consonant. He used John 8:58 as the contrast: in his reading, the issue is that Jesus invokes the divine form rather than the ordinary wording, which is why the hearers understand it as a divine claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=3078" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:18</a> | What does he think of David Rohl's claim that Joseph's tomb has been identified? | He rejected it as unconvincing, arguing the tomb count is overstated, the context is wrong, and the dating is about a century too early for Joseph. | He objected that the statue evidence is exaggerated, that the claimed twelve tombs are actually part of a larger cemetery, and that the supposed palace is another tomb built over other graves. He also said the material culture dates the complex to the Thirteenth Dynasty, roughly a century too early for Joseph, so he sees it as more likely a local Hyksos or high official's tomb than Joseph's. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=3344" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:44</a> | How does Israel's status as the chosen people relate to Messiah's coming and to Messianic believers? | He said Israel still has a special role in salvation history, but Jewish believers still come through grace and faith in Messiah like everyone else and can be especially effective witnesses. | He explained chosenness in terms of Israel being called as a kingdom of priests and first evangelists to the nations, a mission fulfilled through Messiah and the church after Israel failed in it. He still sees Jewish and Israelite believers as having a special role in salvation history, but insisted they come to Messiah the same way as Gentiles, by grace through faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=3575" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:35</a> | If he played D&D, what character class and race would he be, and if not, what is his favorite tomb in Egypt? | He said he used to play a half-elf magic user or wizard, and named several favorite tombs, especially Sennejem, Rekhmire, Ramose, and the Pyramid of Djoser. | He answered the game question by saying he used to play half-elf magic users or wizards. For tombs, he praised Sennejem, Rekhmire, and Ramose, but spent extra time on Djoser's pyramid because its underground burial chamber, galleries, shafts, and maze-like spaces impressed him even though he usually prefers decorated tombs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=3904" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:04</a> | Did the Egyptians adopt foreign gods or identify them with their own deities? | Yes. He said Egypt regularly adopted foreign gods when they fit the wider polytheistic system, and later often merged foreign and Egyptian deities rather than claiming the foreign god had always been Egyptian. | He said Egyptians readily adopted foreign gods when those gods did not disrupt the polytheistic system, giving Bes, Reshef, Baal, and Astarte as examples. In the Ptolemaic period, he said, they might merge Greek and Egyptian gods as versions of the same deity, but usually not by claiming the foreign god had originally been Egyptian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=4200" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:00</a> | Is Leviticus 15:19 about menstrual blood polluting the land? | No. He read it as ritual language about normal and abnormal bodily states, not as a statement that menstrual blood pollutes the land. | He read Leviticus 15 in context with male emissions and intercourse, arguing that the clean/unclean language concerns normal and abnormal bodily states rather than land pollution. He framed the menstrual period as a ritually unclean state for a limited time and even suggested the rule could have functioned as a rest period in a hard ancient labor environment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=4531" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:31</a> | Was the star of Bethlehem present at Jesus' birth, and can it be used to date the birth? | He said the star belongs to the Magi episode after the birth, not the stable scene, so it does not directly give a birth date. | He argued that the Magi arrived well after the birth, when the family was already in a house, so the star belongs to that later visit rather than to the nativity scene. He also rejected using Revelation 12 or other archaeoastronomical arguments to date the birth, preferring Old Testament symbolic readings and saying that even identifying the star would not date the birth itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=4849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:49</a> | What might life have been like for Moses growing up among Egyptians but not as one of them? | He pictured Moses growing up in the Ramesside household at Avaris, exposed both to elite Egyptian administration and to the surrounding Semitic-speaking local population. | He placed Moses in the Ramesside household at the palace of Avaris, which he described as an administrative residence for Lower Egypt rather than the king's main palace. That would have given Moses an elite Egyptian upbringing with access to administration, while still leaving him near the Semitic-speaking local population and not as isolated as a royal child in Thebes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=5164" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:04</a> | How do Exodus 25:9 and Hebrews 8:5 fit with the idea that the tabernacle design drew on Egyptian models? | He said the tabernacle was a copy and shadow adapted to what the Israelites already knew, so God gave a pattern in culturally intelligible terms rather than a de novo heavenly replica. | He emphasized that Hebrews calls the tabernacle a copy and shadow, not a one-to-one heavenly duplicate. In his view, God accommodated the pattern to categories the Israelites already understood from their world, using familiar forms to point toward heavenly realities rather than handing them an alien design they could not interpret or build. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=5617" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:37</a> | Is Genesis 15:4-5 a theophany, and could this be the angel of the Lord? | He said it is very likely a theophany; whether it is the angel of the Lord depends on how broadly that phrase is being used. | He read the passage as the Lord directly manifesting and speaking to Abraham, so he called it very likely a theophany. He allowed that it could also be described as the angel or messenger of the Lord if that term is being used broadly for a divine manifestation, but he treated that second identification as less definite because the wording itself is broader. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=5860" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:40</a> | How could Egypt have hyperinflation in a barter or precious-metal economy? | He said inflation can happen when goods become scarce relative to other goods, and Egypt's loss of tribute and food supply pressures made prices rise across the economy. | He explained inflation by analogy: prices can rise either because currency increases or because the supply of goods falls. In a barter economy, goods still become more expensive relative to other goods when scarcity increases, and he tied Egypt's hyperinflation to lost tribute from Canaan, Kush, and Nubia, food shortages, labor strikes, social strain, and the late Twentieth Dynasty crisis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=6404" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:44</a> | As a manual laborer, does he recommend manual labor for Christians today, and are coworkers more resistant to the world than white-collar workers? | No blanket recommendation. He said his own workplace is spiritually difficult, and manual laborers are not automatically better in character or more resistant to worldly pressures. | He rejected the idea that manual labor automatically produces better character or more resistance to worldly pressures. Speaking from his own experience, he described his workplace as spiritually challenging and said coworkers vary by individual, so he would not romanticize manual labor over academia or white-collar work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=6570" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:30</a> | What is on his necklace? | He said it is his self-made two-tone wedding ring, worn on a chain because weight loss made it no longer fit and the ring cannot easily be resized. | He explained that the necklace holds the wedding ring he made when he was an art jeweler. Because the ring combines rose gold and yellow gold, resizing it is difficult, and after losing significant weight it no longer fit securely on his hand, so he keeps it on a strong steel chain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=6841" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:01</a> | Roughly when in Horemheb's reign would Moses have fled Egypt? | He estimated around the twelfth to fourteenth regnal year of Horemheb, with Moses still comparatively young rather than already forty in a literal sense. | He guessed the twelfth to fourteenth year of Horemheb, near the end of the reign, and linked that to Moses being roughly in his late teens rather than literally forty. He argued Moses needed to be old enough to survive the Sinai wilderness and that Horemheb's law-and-order character would have made a killing by someone in the Ramesside household dangerous rather than protected. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=7030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:10</a> | Who wrote Genesis 1-11, when and where was it written, and why? | He suggested it may come from Moses or his scribes around the Exodus as a nation-building anti-myth drawing on Egyptian, Mesopotamian, and Levantine material. | He did not favor a purely Babylonian-exile origin because he sees Genesis 1-11 as drawing on a wider pool of Mesopotamian, Egyptian, and Levantine motifs that Moses or his scribes could have accessed. He described it as an anti-myth for a mixed multitude leaving Egypt, giving a shared origin story and theological framework to forge many peoples into one nation and prepare them for tabernacle worship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=7574" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:14</a> | If later pharaohs could not build pyramids because of labor costs, why not use slaves or honor-based unpaid labor? | He said slaves and volunteers still had to be fed and supplied, so the real expense remained provisioning labor; later pyramids became cheaper, smaller, or were replaced by rock-cut tombs. | He said slave or volunteer labor was not free in practice because workers still needed food, clothing, and basic support, and in Egypt payment was often food. He pointed to cost-cutting changes such as Middle Kingdom mudbrick pyramids, smaller pyramids, and eventually rock-cut tombs, and tied labor stoppages like Deir el-Medina to disrupted food shipments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=7823" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:23</a> | What are the biggest misconceptions about ancient Egypt, and which one annoys him the most? | He singled out the idea that Egypt had an ironclad empire, arguing that Egyptian power in Canaan was more like tribute hegemony than annexation or tight political control. | He argued that Egypt's control of Canaan was not an annexed, tightly governed empire but a hegemony designed to extract tribute. He used the Amarna letters and small garrison towns to say Egypt mainly wanted tribute collected and reported, with limited troops and bureaucrats enforcing a system more like pressure and extraction than full administrative rule. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=8086" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:46</a> | What Egyptian elements does he see in Genesis 1-11? | He mentioned creation by divine speech, humans formed from earth or clay, and serpent or bronze imagery, while noting that a fuller study would be needed. | He gave examples such as creation by divine speech from Memphite theology, humans formed from earth or clay as in Khnum traditions, and serpent or bronze imagery connected with Egyptian mirror bronze. He said he had not done a full study and also sees Mesopotamian motifs, but he thinks Egyptian motifs are woven through Genesis, especially in and after the opening chapters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=8394" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:54</a> | Does biblical language remind him of a fractal, with parables picturing something greater? | Yes. He connected that effect especially with compressed poetic and prophetic language and recommended repeated meditative reading to see more layers in a passage. | He said the effect is especially visible in Psalms, poetry, prophecy, and sometimes parables because compressed language communicates more than the surface words alone. His practical advice was to reread a short biblical passage several times a day for a month, pushing past boredom until repeated meditation opens up connections and layers that were not obvious at first. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=8717" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:17</a> | Which dynasty of Egypt had the highest percentage of slaves among the population? | He said it is hard to answer, but pointed to high slave-population or slave-capture periods in Dynasties 4, 12, and especially 18-19, with slave collection continuing later. | He cautioned that the question is difficult to answer precisely, then identified major high points for slave populations or slave capture in Dynasties 4, 12, and especially 18 and 19. He added that slave collection continued into later periods, including Dynasties 20, 22, 23, and 25, though he did not rank them with certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=8873" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:53</a> | How did new Egyptian dynasties legitimize themselves religiously? | He said they linked themselves to prestigious ancestors, poured wealth into temples, and at times used royal or prophetic texts to frame their rule as divinely warranted. | He listed several strategies: claiming connection to prestigious ancestors, inserting themselves into royal memory, and making large donations of metal, stones, labor, land, and building projects to temples. He also cited ex eventu prophetic texts connected with Dynasty 12 as literary legitimation, while stressing that temple endowments helped win priestly support. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=9728" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:08</a> | What does he think of depictions of ancient Egypt in fiction such as The Mummy, Gods of Egypt, Yu-Gi-Oh!, Stargate, and Prince of Egypt, and which is his favorite? | He thought Cecil B. DeMille's Ten Commandments was the most accurate popular depiction, while works like The Mummy and Stargate are entertaining but poor Egyptology. | He judged Cecil B. DeMille's Ten Commandments as the most accurate popular film depiction of Egypt he had seen, though he still criticized parts of the film. He described The Mummy and Stargate as entertaining but poor Egyptology, Gods of Egypt as especially bad in costuming, and Prince of Egypt as trying but still inaccurate in places. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=10176" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:36</a> | Will viewers ever see him pick up the Pelham blue SG and shred? | No. He said he lacks a suitable amp and lives in a noise-sensitive apartment, so viewers should not expect that. | He answered flatly that viewers should not expect it. He said he does not have a good enough amp, lives in an apartment with noise-sensitive neighbors, and usually has to play quietly with headphones, so public guitar shredding is not likely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=10248" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:48</a> | What would someone need to get started in bladesmithing? | He said beginners can start with stock removal, pre-made blades, or a small forge, but need metal, shaping tools, a hardening method, and usually an anvil or other suitable setup. | He distinguished knife making from bladesmithing: a beginner might fit handles to pre-made blades, use stock removal by filing or grinding, or build a small forge from refractory bricks with a propane torch. For actual blade work, he said the essentials are metal, a way to shape it, a way to harden it by forge and quench or a service provider, and typically an anvil, with handle materials varying by project. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=10559" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:59</a> | How much did Egyptians and other ancient Near Eastern cultures know about marine life such as the creatures later compared with Leviathan? | He said Egyptians knew more than people assume, especially through Red Sea voyages and depictions of sea life, though they were still primarily a river civilization. | He said Egyptian knowledge of marine life was real despite Egypt being a river culture, because Egyptians undertook Red Sea and Punt expeditions. He pointed to Hatshepsut's Punt reliefs with stingrays, sharks, and other fish, as well as Old Kingdom Red Sea activity such as Wadi el-Jarf, while still saying he was unsure how much fishing they regularly did. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/oz6vqfVDmQM?t=10762" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:22</a> | Is the chronology of the Israelite and Judahite kings basically settled, or does it still need major revision? | He said it may still need minor tweaks, but the overall framework is already very strong and does not need an overhaul. | He said Thiele's chronology improved through successive revisions and that Kenneth Kitchen added further refinements, leaving the framework very accurate overall. He allowed for minor one- or two-year tweaks and one unresolved point, but rejected the idea that the Israelite and Judahite king chronology needs a major overhaul. |
